subtopics.title: K-Means Clustering
subtopics.definition: Partitions data into k distinct clusters based on centroid distances
subtopics.explanation: K-Means groups data into k clusters by minimizing within-cluster variance (inertia). Algorithm: 1) Initialize k centroids randomly, 2) Assign points to nearest centroid, 3) Update centroids to cluster means, 4) Repeat until convergence. Choose k using elbow method (inertia vs k) or silhouette score. Sensitive to initialization (use k-means++), scaling, and outliers. Assumes spherical clusters of similar size.
subtopics.example: Customer segmentation: k=3 clusters (budget, moderate, luxury shoppers) based on spending patterns and frequency.
subtopics.code:
import numpy as np
from sklearn.cluster import KMeans
from sklearn.datasets import make_blobs
from sklearn.metrics import silhouette_score
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

# Generate sample data
X, y = make_blobs(n_samples=300, centers=4, cluster_std=0.6, random_state=42)

print("🔷 K-Means Clustering")
print("=" * 60)

# Elbow method to find optimal k
inertias = []
K_range = range(1, 11)
for k in K_range:
    kmeans = KMeans(n_clusters=k, random_state=42, n_init=10)
    kmeans.fit(X)
    inertias.append(kmeans.inertia_)

print("\n📊 Elbow Method:")
for k, inert in zip(K_range, inertias):
    print(f"  k={k}: inertia={inert:.2f}")

# Optimal k from elbow (look for bend)
optimal_k = 4
kmeans = KMeans(n_clusters=optimal_k, random_state=42, n_init=10)
labels = kmeans.fit_predict(X)
silhouette = silhouette_score(X, labels)

print(f"\n📊 K-Means Results (k={optimal_k}):")
print(f"  Inertia: {kmeans.inertia_:.2f}")
print(f"  Silhouette Score: {silhouette:.4f}")
print(f"  Cluster centers: {kmeans.cluster_centers_.shape}")

print("\n✅ K-Means Properties:")
print("  • Fast and scalable (O(n·k·d·iter))")
print("  • Assumes spherical clusters of equal size")
print("  • Sensitive to outliers and scaling")
print("  • Use k-means++ for better initialization")
print("\n💡 Choosing k:")
print("  • Elbow method: Look for bend in inertia curve")
print("  • Silhouette score: Maximize average silhouette")
print("  • Domain knowledge: Business requirements")

subtopics.title: Hierarchical Clustering
subtopics.explanation: Hierarchical clustering creates nested clusters without pre-specifying k. Two approaches: Agglomerative (bottom-up): each point starts as its own cluster, repeatedly merge closest clusters. Divisive (top-down): start with one cluster, recursively split. Linkage methods: single (min distance), complete (max distance), average, Ward (min variance). Dendrogram visualizes merging history. Cut dendrogram at desired height to get clusters. O(n³) time complexity, not scalable to large datasets.
subtopics.example: Biology taxonomy: species grouped into genus, family, order based on genetic similarity, shown as dendrogram.
subtopics.code:
from sklearn.cluster import AgglomerativeClustering
from scipy.cluster.hierarchy import dendrogram, linkage
from scipy.spatial.distance import pdist

print("\n🔷 Hierarchical Clustering")
print("=" * 60)

# Generate sample data
X_hier, _ = make_blobs(n_samples=50, centers=3, cluster_std=0.8, random_state=42)

# Compute linkage matrix
linkage_matrix = linkage(X_hier, method='ward')

print("\n📊 Linkage Methods:")
print("  • Single: min distance between clusters (forms chains)")
print("  • Complete: max distance between clusters (compact clusters)")
print("  • Average: average distance between clusters")
print("  • Ward: minimize within-cluster variance (default)")

# Agglomerative clustering
for n_clusters in [2, 3, 4]:
    hier = AgglomerativeClustering(n_clusters=n_clusters, linkage='ward')
    labels = hier.fit_predict(X_hier)
    sil = silhouette_score(X_hier, labels)
    print(f"\n  n_clusters={n_clusters}: silhouette={sil:.4f}")

print("\n✅ Hierarchical Clustering Properties:")
print("  • No need to pre-specify k")
print("  • Dendrogram provides interpretable visualization")
print("  • O(n³) time complexity (not for large datasets)")
print("  • Deterministic (no random initialization)")
print("\n💡 Linkage Choice:")
print("  • Ward: Compact, spherical clusters")
print("  • Single: Finds arbitrary shapes (chaining)")
print("  • Complete: Tight, compact clusters")

subtopics.title: DBSCAN (Density-Based Spatial Clustering)
subtopics.definition: Groups points based on density connectivity
subtopics.explanation: DBSCAN finds clusters of arbitrary shape by identifying dense regions separated by sparse areas. Parameters: eps (neighborhood radius), min_samples (minimum points to form dense region). Points classified as: core (≥min_samples within eps), border (within eps of core), noise (neither). Advantages: finds arbitrary shapes, handles outliers automatically, no need to specify k. Disadvantages: sensitive to eps/min_samples, struggles with varying densities.
subtopics.example: Geospatial clustering: identifying crime hotspots (dense areas) with isolated points as outliers.
subtopics.code:
from sklearn.cluster import DBSCAN
from sklearn.datasets import make_moons

print("\n🔷 DBSCAN (Density-Based Clustering)")
print("=" * 60)

# Generate non-spherical data
X_moons, _ = make_moons(n_samples=200, noise=0.05, random_state=42)

# Apply DBSCAN with different parameters
eps_values = [0.1, 0.2, 0.3]
min_samples_values = [3, 5, 10]

print("\n📊 DBSCAN Parameters:")
print("  eps: Neighborhood radius (small = many noise points)")
print("  min_samples: Minimum points to form dense region")

for eps in eps_values:
    for min_samples in min_samples_values:
        dbscan = DBSCAN(eps=eps, min_samples=min_samples)
        labels = dbscan.fit_predict(X_moons)
        n_clusters = len(set(labels)) - (1 if -1 in labels else 0)
        n_noise = np.sum(labels == -1)
        print(f"  eps={eps}, min_samples={min_samples}: clusters={n_clusters}, noise={n_noise}")

# Optimal parameters
best_dbscan = DBSCAN(eps=0.2, min_samples=5)
labels_best = best_dbscan.fit_predict(X_moons)
n_clusters = len(set(labels_best)) - (1 if -1 in labels_best else 0)
n_noise = np.sum(labels_best == -1)

print(f"\n📊 DBSCAN Results (eps=0.2, min_samples=5):")
print(f"  Number of clusters: {n_clusters}")
print(f"  Noise points: {n_noise}")
print(f"  Unique labels: {set(labels_best)}")

print("\n✅ DBSCAN Properties:")
print("  • Finds arbitrary-shaped clusters")
print("  • Handles outliers automatically (noise points)")
print("  • No need to specify number of clusters")
print("  • Sensitive to eps and min_samples")
print("\n💡 Parameter Tuning:")
print("  • eps: Use k-distance graph (elbow point)")
print("  • min_samples: Typically 2·dim (e.g., 4 for 2D)")
print("  • Start with small eps, increase until clusters form")

subtopics.title: PCA (Principal Component Analysis)
subtopics.explanation: PCA finds orthogonal directions (principal components) that maximize variance in data. Projects data onto top k components, reducing dimensions while preserving most variance. Components are eigenvectors of covariance matrix, eigenvalues indicate variance explained. Steps: standardize data, compute covariance, eigen decomposition, select top components. Applications: visualization (2D/3D), noise reduction, feature extraction, compression. Limitations: linear only, assumes Gaussian distribution, components are less interpretable.
subtopics.example: 100-dimensional image data reduced to 50 dimensions while preserving 95% variance, enabling faster training and visualization.
subtopics.code:
from sklearn.decomposition import PCA
from sklearn.preprocessing import StandardScaler
from sklearn.datasets import load_digits

print("\n🔷 PCA (Principal Component Analysis)")
print("=" * 60)

# Load digits dataset (64 dimensions)
digits = load_digits()
X_digits = digits.data
y_digits = digits.target

print(f"\n📊 Original Data: {X_digits.shape} (64 dimensions)")

# Standardize
scaler = StandardScaler()
X_scaled = scaler.fit_transform(X_digits)

# Apply PCA with different components
for n_comp in [2, 10, 20, 30, 40, 50]:
    pca = PCA(n_components=n_comp)
    X_pca = pca.fit_transform(X_scaled)
    explained_var = pca.explained_variance_ratio_.sum()
    print(f"  {n_comp:2d} components: explained variance = {explained_var:.4f}")

# Find components to retain 95% variance
pca_95 = PCA(n_components=0.95)
X_pca_95 = pca_95.fit_transform(X_scaled)
print(f"\n📊 Components to retain 95% variance: {pca_95.n_components_}")

# Explained variance ratio
pca_full = PCA()
pca_full.fit(X_scaled)
cumsum_var = np.cumsum(pca_full.explained_variance_ratio_)
print(f"\n📊 Cumulative Explained Variance:")
for i, cum in enumerate(cumsum_var[:10], 1):
    print(f"  {i:2d} components: {cum:.4f}")

# 2D visualization
pca_2d = PCA(n_components=2)
X_2d = pca_2d.fit_transform(X_scaled)
print(f"\n📊 2D Projection: {X_2d.shape}")
print(f"  Variance explained by PC1: {pca_2d.explained_variance_ratio_[0]:.4f}")
print(f"  Variance explained by PC2: {pca_2d.explained_variance_ratio_[1]:.4f}")

print("\n✅ PCA Properties:")
print("  • Linear transformation preserving maximum variance")
print("  • Components are orthogonal (uncorrelated)")
print("  • Reduces dimensionality for visualization")
print("  • Helps with multicollinearity")
print("\n💡 Choosing Number of Components:")
print("  • Explained variance threshold (e.g., 95%)")
print("  • Elbow in scree plot (variance vs components)")
print("  • Downstream task performance (e.g., classification accuracy)")
print("\n🔧 PCA for Visualization:")
print("  from sklearn.decomposition import PCA")
print("  pca = PCA(n_components=2)")
print("  X_vis = pca.fit_transform(X_scaled)")
print("  plt.scatter(X_vis[:,0], X_vis[:,1], c=y)")

print("\n📊 Unsupervised Learning Summary:")
print("-" * 50)
print("  Method      | Type          | Best For                     | Scalability")
print("  ------------|---------------|------------------------------|-------------")
print("  K-Means     | Partitional   | Spherical, equal-sized       | O(n·k·d)")
print("  Hierarchical| Tree-based    | Interpretable, small data    | O(n³)")
print("  DBSCAN      | Density-based | Arbitrary shapes, outliers   | O(n·d)")
print("  PCA         | Dimensionality| Visualization, noise reduction| O(d³)")
